data.topic: expressions operators null empty string evaluation
data.content:
### join - Concatenate Array Elements into String

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

Concatenates all values in an array or string into a single string using an optional separator. Defaults to comma separator if no separator is specified. Casts all values to strings before concatenation.

```expression
join(github.event.issue.labels.*.name, ', ')
```

--------------------------------

### Define Literal Data Types in GitHub Actions Expressions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

This example demonstrates how to define various literal data types (null, boolean, number, string) directly within GitHub Actions expressions. It shows syntax for different number formats (integer, float, hex, exponential) and proper string handling, including escaping single quotes.

```yaml
env:
  myNull: ${{ null }}
  myBoolean: ${{ false }}
  myIntegerNumber: ${{ 711 }}
  myFloatNumber: ${{ -9.2 }}
  myHexNumber: ${{ 0xff }}
  myExponentialNumber: ${{ -2.99e-2 }}
  myString: Mona the Octocat
  myStringInBraces: ${{ 'It''s open source!' }}

```

--------------------------------

### fromJSON - Convert String to JSON Object or Data Type

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

Parses JSON strings and converts them to native JSON objects or data types including strings, booleans, null values, arrays, and objects. Enables dynamic matrix strategies and type conversion in workflow expressions.

```yaml
name: build
on: push
jobs:
  job1:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      matrix: ${{ steps.set-matrix.outputs.matrix }}
    steps:
      - id: set-matrix
        run: echo "matrix={\"include\":[{\"project\":\"foo\",\"config\":\"Debug\"},{\"project\":\"bar\",\"config\":\"Release\"}]}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
  job2:
    needs: job1
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix: ${{ fromJSON(needs.job1.outputs.matrix) }}
    steps:
      - run: echo "Matrix - Project ${{ matrix.project }}, Config ${{ matrix.config }}"
```

```yaml
name: print
on: push
env:
  continue: true
  time: 3
jobs:
  job1:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - continue-on-error: ${{ fromJSON(env.continue) }}
        timeout-minutes: ${{ fromJSON(env.time) }}
        run: echo ...
```

--------------------------------

### format - Replace Placeholders in Strings

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

Replaces numbered placeholders ({N}) in a string with corresponding replacement values. Supports unlimited replacement values and allows escaping curly braces with double braces. Useful for dynamic string construction in workflows.

```expression
format('Hello {0} {1} {2}', 'Mona', 'the', 'Octocat')
```

```expression
format('{{Hello {0} {1} {2}!}}', 'Mona', 'the', 'Octocat')
```

--------------------------------

### endsWith - Check String Suffix in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

Case-insensitive function that returns true when a string ends with a specified search value. Automatically casts values to strings for comparison. Useful for validating string endings in workflow conditions.

```expression
endsWith('Hello world', 'ld')
```

--------------------------------

### Format String with Placeholders (GitHub Actions)

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/writing-workflows/choosing-what-your-workflow-does/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

This function replaces `{N}` placeholders in a `string` with corresponding `replaceValueN` arguments. It requires at least one `replaceValue` and supports multiple variables.

```GitHub Actions Expression
format('Hello {0} {1} {2}', 'Mona', 'the', 'Octocat')
```

--------------------------------

### startsWith - Check String Prefix in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

Case-insensitive function that returns true when a string starts with a specified search value. Automatically casts values to strings for comparison. Useful for conditional logic based on string prefixes.

```expression
startsWith('Hello world', 'He')
```

--------------------------------

### Object filter syntax with asterisk operator

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

Uses the * syntax to filter and select matching items from arrays or objects in collections. When applied to arrays of objects, returns a new array of values from the specified property. When applied to objects, returns an array of values from all matching properties; order is not guaranteed.

```json
[
  { "name": "apple", "quantity": 1 },
  { "name": "orange", "quantity": 2 },
  { "name": "pear", "quantity": 1 }
]

fruits.*.name returns [ "apple", "orange", "pear" ]
```

```json
{
  "scallions": {
    "colors": ["green", "white", "red"],
    "ediblePortions": ["roots", "stalks"]
  },
  "beets": {
    "colors": ["purple", "red", "gold", "white", "pink"],
    "ediblePortions": ["roots", "stems", "leaves"]
  },
  "artichokes": {
    "colors": ["green", "purple", "red", "black"],
    "ediblePortions": ["hearts", "stems", "leaves"]
  }
}

vegetables.*.ediblePortions returns [
  ["roots", "stalks"],
  ["hearts", "stems", "leaves"],
  ["roots", "stems", "leaves"]
]
```

--------------------------------

### Implement Conditional Logic using Binary Operators in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

This example illustrates how to use binary logical operators (`&&`, `||`) to create conditional assignments for environment variables in GitHub Actions workflows. This pattern mimics a ternary operator, setting `MY_ENV_VAR` based on whether `github.ref` matches 'refs/heads/main'. It's important to ensure the first value after `&&` is truthy for this pattern to work as expected.

```yaml
env:
  MY_ENV_VAR: ${{ github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && 'value_for_main_branch' || 'value_for_other_branches' }}

```

--------------------------------

### Escape Braces in Formatted String (GitHub Actions)

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/writing-workflows/choosing-what-your-workflow-does/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

Demonstrates how to escape curly braces within the `format` function using double braces (`{{` and `}}`). This prevents them from being interpreted as placeholders.

```GitHub Actions Expression
format('{{Hello {0} {1} {2}!}}', 'Mona', 'the', 'Octocat')
```

--------------------------------

### Convert String to JSON Data Type (GitHub Actions)

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

The `fromJSON` function allows conversion of a string `value` into a specific JSON data type, such as boolean or integer. This is useful for dynamic control of workflow properties like `continue-on-error` or `timeout-minutes` based on string-based environment variables.

```yaml
name: print
on: push
env:
  continue: true
  time: 3
jobs:
  job1:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - continue-on-error: ${{ fromJSON(env.continue) }}
        timeout-minutes: ${{ fromJSON(env.time) }}
        run: echo ...
```

--------------------------------

### Convert String to JSON Object for Matrix (GitHub Actions)

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/writing-workflows/choosing-what-your-workflow-does/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

This example shows how to convert a JSON string output from one job into a JSON object using `fromJSON` to dynamically define a matrix strategy for a subsequent job. It enables passing structured data between workflow steps.

```yaml
name: build
on: push
jobs:
  job1:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      matrix: ${{ steps.set-matrix.outputs.matrix }}
    steps:
      - id: set-matrix
        run: echo "matrix={\"include\":[{\"project\":\"foo\",\"config\":\"Debug\"},{\"project\":\"bar\",\"config\":\"Release\"}]}}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
  job2:
    needs: job1
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix: ${{ fromJSON(needs.job1.outputs.matrix) }}
    steps:
      - run: echo "Matrix - Project ${{ matrix.project }}, Config ${{ matrix.config }}"
```

--------------------------------

### Check if an array contains a value in GitHub Actions using `contains` and `fromJSON`

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

This example shows how to efficiently check if a variable's value is present in a predefined list of strings using the `contains()` function combined with `fromJSON()`. It converts a JSON string array into an array for comparison, checking if `github.event_name` is 'push' or 'pull_request'.

```expression
contains(fromJSON('["push", "pull_request"]'), github.event_name)
```

--------------------------------

### hashFiles Function - Single Pattern Matching

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

Generates a SHA-256 hash for files matching a single glob pattern. Returns empty string if no files match. Supports case-insensitive matching on Windows and relative paths within GITHUB_WORKSPACE.

```yaml
hashFiles('**/package-lock.json')
hashFiles('/src/*.js')
hashFiles('/lib/**/*.rb')
```

--------------------------------

### toJSON - Convert Value to Pretty-Print JSON

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

Converts any value to a formatted JSON representation for debugging purposes. Useful for inspecting complex context objects and understanding available data in workflow expressions.

```expression
toJSON(job)
```

--------------------------------

### GitHub Actions contains() function - expression examples

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/writing-workflows/choosing-what-your-workflow-does/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

Demonstrates the built-in contains() function for string searches, object property filtering, and array matching in GitHub Actions expressions. Shows string substring matching, filtering GitHub event labels by name, and using contains() with fromJSON() to check if an array contains a specific value.

```plaintext
contains('Hello world', 'llo')
contains(github.event.issue.labels.*.name, 'bug')
contains(fromJSON('["push", "pull_request"]'), github.event_name)
```

--------------------------------

### Conditional Expressions in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/migrating-to-github-actions/manually-migrating-to-github-actions/migrating-from-azure-pipelines-to-github-actions

Execute steps conditionally using GitHub Actions `if` key with infix operator syntax. The example uses `==` and `&&` operators to evaluate environment variable conditions. Conditional expressions must be wrapped in `${{ }}` template syntax.

```yaml
jobs:
  conditional:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - run: echo "This step runs with str equals 'ABC' and num equals 123"
        if: ${{ env.str == 'ABC' && env.num == 123 }}
```

--------------------------------

### cancelled - Check if workflow was canceled

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

Returns true if the workflow execution was canceled. Used in conditional expressions to run steps only when a workflow cancellation occurs.

```yaml
if: ${{ cancelled() }}
```

--------------------------------

### Object Filter Syntax - Object Property Filtering

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

Applies wildcard filter to object properties to extract nested arrays or values. Order of output is not guaranteed as objects do not preserve insertion order.

```json
{
  "scallions": { "colors": ["green", "white", "red"], "ediblePortions": ["roots", "stalks"] },
  "beets": { "colors": ["purple", "red", "gold", "white", "pink"], "ediblePortions": ["roots", "stems", "leaves"] },
  "artichokes": { "colors": ["green", "purple", "red", "black"], "ediblePortions": ["hearts", "stems", "leaves"] }
}

vegetables.*.ediblePortions returns [ ["roots", "stalks"], ["hearts", "stems", "leaves"], ["roots", "stems", "leaves"] ]
```

--------------------------------

### Parse JSON String to Object (GitHub Actions)

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

The `fromJSON` function converts a JSON string `value` into a JSON object or other specified JSON data type. This example demonstrates how to pass a JSON object, like a matrix configuration, between jobs in a workflow using outputs.

```yaml
name: build
on: push
jobs:
  job1:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      matrix: ${{ steps.set-matrix.outputs.matrix }}
    steps:
      - id: set-matrix
        run: echo "matrix={"include":[{"project":"foo","config":"Debug"},{"project":"bar","config":"Release"}]}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
  job2:
    needs: job1
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix: ${{ fromJSON(needs.job1.outputs.matrix) }}
    steps:
      - run: echo "Matrix - Project ${{ matrix.project }}, Config ${{ matrix.config }}"
```

--------------------------------

### Check string containment with contains() function

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/expressions

Examples of using the contains() function in GitHub Actions to perform case-insensitive substring and array element searches. Can be used to check if strings contain substrings or if arrays contain specific items.

```text
contains('Hello world', 'llo') returns true
contains(github.event.issue.labels.*.name, 'bug') returns true if issue has "bug" label
contains(fromJSON('["push", "pull_request"]'), github.event_name) returns true if event_name is "push" or "pull_request"
```

--------------------------------

### Object Filter Syntax - Array Filtering

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

Uses the * wildcard syntax to extract matching properties from array elements. Applies a filter across all objects in a collection and returns an array of selected values.

```json
[
  { "name": "apple", "quantity": 1 },
  { "name": "orange", "quantity": 2 },
  { "name": "pear", "quantity": 1 }
]

fruits.*.name returns [ "apple", "orange", "pear" ]
```

--------------------------------

### success - Status check function for successful steps

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

Returns true when all previous steps in a job have succeeded. Used as a conditional expression in if statements to determine if a step should execute. This is the default status check applied when no other status function is specified.

```yaml
steps:
  ...
  - name: The job has succeeded
    if: ${{ success() }}
```

--------------------------------

### Object Filters - Wildcard Syntax

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

Use the asterisk (*) syntax to apply filters and select matching items in collections. Works on arrays of objects to extract specific properties or on objects to access nested values across all keys.

```APIDOC
## Object Filters - Wildcard Syntax

### Description
Use the `*` syntax to apply filters and select matching items in arrays of objects or to access properties across all keys in an object.

### Syntax
```
collection.*.property
object.*.nestedProperty
```

### Array Filter Example

Given an array of objects named `fruits`:
```json
[
  { "name": "apple", "quantity": 1 },
  { "name": "orange", "quantity": 2 },
  { "name": "pear", "quantity": 1 }
]
```

The filter `fruits.*.name` returns:
```json
["apple", "orange", "pear"]
```

### Object Filter Example

Given an object named `vegetables`:
```json
{
  "scallions": {
    "colors": ["green", "white", "red"],
    "ediblePortions": ["roots", "stalks"]
  },
  "beets": {
    "colors": ["purple", "red", "gold", "white", "pink"],
    "ediblePortions": ["roots", "stems", "leaves"]
  },
  "artichokes": {
    "colors": ["green", "purple", "red", "black"],
    "ediblePortions": ["hearts", "stems", "leaves"]
  }
}
```

The filter `vegetables.*.ediblePortions` returns:
```json
[
  ["roots", "stalks"],
  ["hearts", "stems", "leaves"],
  ["roots", "stems", "leaves"]
]
```

### Notes
- Objects do not preserve order, so output order cannot be guaranteed
- Filters return arrays even when applied to single objects
- Useful for extracting data from matrix builds and dynamic job contexts
```

--------------------------------

### hashFiles Function

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

Calculates a SHA-256 hash for a set of files matching the provided path pattern(s). Returns a single hash for the entire set of matched files, or an empty string if no files match. Supports glob pattern matching with case-insensitive matching on Windows.

```APIDOC
## hashFiles Function

### Description
Returns a single SHA-256 hash for the set of files that matches the path pattern. Accepts single or multiple comma-separated path patterns relative to GITHUB_WORKSPACE. Calculates individual SHA-256 hashes for each matched file, then combines them into a final hash.

### Syntax
```
hashFiles(path)
```

### Parameters
- **path** (string) - Required - Single glob pattern or comma-separated patterns relative to GITHUB_WORKSPACE. Supports standard glob matching characters.

### Returns
- **hash** (string) - SHA-256 hash of the matched files, or empty string if no files match the pattern

### Single Pattern Examples

#### Match package-lock.json anywhere in repository
```
hashFiles('**/package-lock.json')
```

#### Match .js files in src directory (root level only)
```
hashFiles('src/*.js')
```

#### Match .rb files in lib directory (including subdirectories)
```
hashFiles('lib/**/*.rb')
```

### Multiple Pattern Examples

#### Hash package-lock.json and Gemfile.lock
```
hashFiles('**/package-lock.json', '**/Gemfile.lock')
```

#### Hash .rb files in lib excluding foo subdirectory
```
hashFiles('lib/**/*.rb', '!/lib/foo/*.rb')
```
```

--------------------------------

### Sample Data - Fruits Array

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/writing-workflows/choosing-what-your-workflow-does/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

Example array of fruit objects with name and quantity properties used to demonstrate array filtering syntax.

```json
[
  { "name": "apple", "quantity": 1 },
  { "name": "orange", "quantity": 2 },
  { "name": "pear", "quantity": 1 }
]
```

--------------------------------

### Check for substring presence in GitHub Actions using `contains` function

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

This example demonstrates the usage of the `contains()` function in GitHub Actions expressions to check if a string contains a specified substring. It returns `true` if 'llo' is found within 'Hello world'. This function is not case sensitive.

```expression
contains('Hello world', 'llo')
```

--------------------------------

### cancelled() Function

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

Returns `true` if the workflow run was canceled at any point. This can be used in 'if' conditionals to perform cleanup or specific actions upon cancellation.

```APIDOC
## cancelled()

### Description
Returns `true` if the workflow run was canceled. This function is useful for performing cleanup or specific actions only when a workflow is interrupted.

### Method
FUNCTION

### Parameters
N/A

### Example Usage
```yaml
steps:
  - name: A long-running task
    run: sleep 60
  - name: Cleanup on cancellation
    if: ${{ cancelled() }}
    run: echo "Workflow was cancelled. Performing cleanup..."
```

### Returns
(boolean) - `true` if the workflow was canceled, `false` otherwise.
```

--------------------------------

### Sample Data - Vegetables Object

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/writing-workflows/choosing-what-your-workflow-does/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

Example nested object structure with vegetable properties including colors and edible portions used to demonstrate object filtering syntax.

```json
{
  "scallions": {
    "colors": ["green", "white", "red"],
    "ediblePortions": ["roots", "stalks"]
  },
  "beets": {
    "colors": ["purple", "red", "gold", "white", "pink"],
    "ediblePortions": ["roots", "stems", "leaves"]
  },
  "artichokes": {
    "colors": ["green", "purple", "red", "black"],
    "ediblePortions": ["hearts", "stems", "leaves"]
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Object Filters - Object Property Filtering

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/expressions

Apply * syntax to objects to extract property values from all nested objects. Returns array of values for specified property across all object keys. Order is not guaranteed.

```json
{
  "scallions": {
    "colors": ["green", "white", "red"],
    "ediblePortions": ["roots", "stalks"]
  },
  "beets": {
    "colors": ["purple", "red", "gold", "white", "pink"],
    "ediblePortions": ["roots", "stems", "leaves"]
  },
  "artichokes": {
    "colors": ["green", "purple", "red", "black"],
    "ediblePortions": ["hearts", "stems", "leaves"]
  }
}

vegetables.*.ediblePortions returns [
  ["roots", "stalks"],
  ["hearts", "stems", "leaves"],
  ["roots", "stems", "leaves"]
]
```

--------------------------------

### Object Filter Syntax - Object Property Extraction

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/writing-workflows/choosing-what-your-workflow-does/evaluate-expressions-in-workflows-and-actions

Apply * filter on object properties to extract values from nested structures. Returns array of matching property values across all object keys. Order of output not guaranteed due to object property ordering.

```json
vegetables.*.ediblePortions
```

--------------------------------

### Use if conditional with expression syntax in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/metadata-syntax

Demonstrates how to use the `if` conditional to prevent a step from running unless a condition is met. The `if` conditional evaluates expressions using GitHub Actions contexts. When expressions start with `!`, you must use the `${{ }}` expression syntax to avoid YAML format conflicts.

```yaml
if: ${{ ! startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/') }}
```

--------------------------------

### always() Function

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

Causes a step to always execute, regardless of the success or failure of previous steps, and even if the workflow run has been canceled. Use with caution to avoid hanging workflows on critical failures.

```APIDOC
## always()

### Description
Returns `true` and causes the step to always execute, even if previous steps failed or the workflow was canceled. This is best used at the step level or for tasks that must run irrespective of job outcomes, such as sending logs.

### Method
FUNCTION

### Parameters
N/A

### Example Usage
```yaml
steps:
  - name: Critical step that might fail
    run: exit 1
  - name: This step always runs
    if: ${{ always() }}
    run: echo "This message will always appear, even if the previous step failed or the job was cancelled."
```

### Returns
(boolean) - Always `true`.
```

--------------------------------

### failure() Function

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions

Returns `true` if any previous step in the current job has failed. For dependent jobs, it also returns `true` if any ancestor job in the dependency chain has failed.

```APIDOC
## failure()

### Description
Returns `true` if any previous step within the current job fails. If there is a chain of dependent jobs, it will also return `true` if any ancestor job fails. This function overrides the default `success()` check.

### Method
FUNCTION

### Parameters
N/A

### Example Usage
```yaml
steps:
  - name: Step that is expected to fail
    id: failing_step
    run: exit 1
  - name: React to failure
    if: ${{ failure() }}
    run: echo "A previous step failed!"
  - name: React to specific step failure
    if: ${{ failure() && steps.failing_step.conclusion == 'failure' }}
    run: echo "The 'failing_step' specifically failed."
```

### Returns
(boolean) - `true` if any previous step or ancestor job failed, `false` otherwise.
```

--------------------------------

### Set Environment Variable with Expression in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/concepts/workflows-and-actions/expressions

Sets an environment variable using GitHub Actions expression syntax. The expression is evaluated at runtime and the result is assigned to the environment variable. This example demonstrates the basic syntax pattern for including expressions in workflow files.

```yaml
env:
  MY_ENV_VAR: ${{ <expression> }}
```

params.topic: expressions operators null empty string evaluation
